---
title: "The Perceptron"
date: "2026-03-14"
author: "Julian M. Del Fiore"
categories: [classification]
---

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
activation = z => z >= 0 ? 1 : 0;
```

The perceptron is a classification algorithm, so given an input $x$, we want to produce a label $\hat{y}$.
In particular, it is a supervised model, meaning that we will have a labelled training set, where we know the true value $y$ for each sample.

In general, we can choose how to represent $y$.
For binary classification problems, some typical choices include $\{0, 1\}$ and $\{-1, 1\}$.
Using one or the other does not affect the generality of the conclusions.
We will alternate between both, selecting the representation that leads to the easiest interpretation in each section.

## Predicting

We first need to understand how, given an input $x$, the model predicts a label $\hat{y}$.
In short, this happens:

1. using a linear transformation
2. selecting an activation function
3. determinining a classification criteria
4. identifying the decision regions based on the criteria

Next we dive into each of these steps.

### Linear transformation

As we get a sample as input, the value of the features $x$ represent the "concrete knowledge" we have about this example.
Ultimately, we hope that this knowledge will allow us to classify $x$ correctly.

The question is how we want to combine all this information we have of $x$ in a meaningful way.
In particuar, we would love to synthesise all the knowledge we have about $x$ into a unique measure.

Similar to what is done in many other models, the perceptron uses a linear transformation for that

$$z = wx + b$$

where

 * $x$ is the input, and;
 * $w$ and $b$ are parameters of the model, the weight and bias respectively.

With this linear transformation, $x$ could be of any size, but $z$ is always just a plain number.
From a mathematical point of view, the trasformation we apply projects the input space $x$ onto a one-dimensional space defined by $z$.

### Activation function

<!-- The activation function determines how the model’s internal score is turned into an output — whether as a probability, a decision, or a non-linear response. -->

With the linear transformation, we condense the knowledge of $x$ into $z$.
But as this happens, we now need a way of understanding what $z$ is trying to tell us.

To complete that piece of the puzzle, the $z$-value is sent through an activation function $\activation(z)$.
In the case of the perceptron, the $\sgn(z)$ or _step_ function show in @fig-sign is used

$$
\activation(z) = \sgn(z) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-sign}

![Activation function $\sgn(z)$.](/assets/classification/perceptron/figures/sign.png){#fig-sign width=50%}

### Classification criteria

Finally, we need to tell whether we map $x$ to either $\hat{y} = 0$ or $\hat{y} = 1$.

In the perceptron, the activation function itself produces discrete outputs, so it directly defines the classification rule

$$\hat{y} = \sgn(z) = \activation(z) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-dec-z}

We typically place a decision threshold in some value $z = z_0$.
This threshold divides the real line into two regions, each corresponding to a predicted class.

In the perceptron, the decision threshold lies in $z_0 = 0$.
This can be seen in @fig-decision-region.

![The decision threshold lies at $z = 0$ (the shaded regions in 2D are just to reinforce the idea).](/assets/classification/perceptron/figures/decision_region_zspace.png){#fig-decision-region width=65%}

### Decision regions and boundary

The @eq-dec-z can be re-written in terms of $x$ as 

$$\hat{y} =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } w x + b \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

We can see that the decision threshold in $z = 0$ corresponds to a geometric boundary in the input $x$-space

$$z_0 = 0 \Leftrightarrow w x_0 + b = 0$$

The formula $w x_0 + b = 0$ defines what we typically call an hyperplane.
Focusing on $x \in \mathbb{R}$ for now, we can find $x_0$ directly as $x_0 = -\frac{b}{w}$.

In @fig-decision-boundary-1d, we can choose $w$ and $b$, and the animation displays where $x_0$ would lie for the given combination.

::: {#fig-decision-boundary-1d}
{{< include ../_shared/decision-plot_1d.qmd >}}
Decision boundary — adjust $w$ and $b$ to explore different configurations.
:::

## Training

The perceptron is a supervised algorithm.
This means that during training, we need to have labeled samples.
Assuming that's the case, for any training example $x$, we know the true label $y$.

For each example we run through our classifier, we get a prediction $\hat{y}$.
Since we know the true labels, we are able to compare $\hat{y}$ against the expected $y$.
The more times $\hat{y} = y$, the better our model is performing.

The training is the phase in which we refine the predictions $\hat{y}$ that our classifier makes.
For that to be possible, we need to update the parameters $w$ and $b$.

The hope is that, iterating through the training set, we will learn good values for $w$ and $b$, so that our predictions $\hat{y}$ are mostly correct.

To refine the values of $w$ and $b$, we need a strategy to know how to proceed.
The way this typically goes is that define a a loss function, and try to minimise it. 

### Loss function

We want our model to make the best predictions on average.
By "on average", we mean that, given many samples, we would love most of our predictions to be right.

To express this desire in a mathematical form, we can create a function. 
The value our function takes would depend how close our predictions were to the true labels we were trying to predict. 

There are two options for our function:

* we could measure the goodness of fit, i.e. quantify how well our model predicts each sample, and aim to to maximise the value of that function, or;
* we could calculate the mismatch between our predictions and their expected values, and attempt to minimise this difference. 

In ML we typically model problems in the latter way, and talk about a _loss_ or _cost_ function.
The problem now becomes an optimisation one, where we want to minimise the cost function. 
How do we do that? Tuning the parameters of our model, namely $w$ and $b$.

Assuming $\hat{y} \in \{-1, 1\}$ in the following (no loss of generality, just nicer formulas), the loss function used in the perceptron is

$$
\loss = \max(0, -yz) =
\begin{cases}
-yz & \text{if } -  yz \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

Overall, the loss function has the shape of a function $\max(0, x)$, only that the value of $y$ modulates whether the plot gets flipped or not.
This is more clearly shown in in @fig-loss.

::: {#fig-loss}
{{< include ../_shared/loss-perceptron.qmd >}}
Plot of the loss function $\loss = \max(0, -yz)$
:::

### Interpreting the loss function

In essence, function $\max(0, -yz)$ is mainly looking at whether $z$ has the correct sign, i.e. whether $y$ and $z$ are both simultaneously either positive or negative.

Recall that the classification criteria is such that for $z \ge 0$ we assign $\hat{y} = 1$.
With that it mind:

* if $y = 1$, then the classification is correct if $z \ge 0$. 
* if instead $y = -1$, then the classification is correct if $z < 0$.

Independently of the value of $y$, what is common in both cases is that sign of $y$ and $z$ is always the same when the classification is correct, i.e. it holds that $yz \ge 0$. 

The previous condition is equivalent to saying that $- yz < 0$ whenever our model is right.
The maximum between this negative number and 0 is 0. 
Therefore, the cost $\loss$ in these cases is exactly 0.

For mistakes, the analysis is pretty much the same, only that $- yz \ge 0$ and therefore the loss function will be exactly $- yz$.

So overall, $\loss = \max(0, -yz)$ is just a very compact formula that allows to assign no cost for correct classifications, and a cost proportional to how far we are from changing our guess in mistakes.

### Updating the model parameters

To minimise the loss function, we update the model parameters. 
The question then is how to exactly. 

In the case of the perceptron, the optimisation strategy is to use gradient descent. 
If our loss function has a global minimum, then we know that as long as we update the parameters in a way that the value of the loss decreases, we are moving in the right direction. 

There could still be many candidate directions in which the value of the loss decreases, so we still need to decide which one to use. 
To answer that, gradient descent is smart and proposes to move in the direction opposite to the gradient. 

The gradient is the direction of greatest increase, so moving in its opposite direction actually leads to the function's greatest decrease. 
Hence, this direction is not only optimal in this sense, but also we can systematically compute it using partial derivatives

$$
\nabla \loss(w,b) =
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{\partial \loss}{\partial w} \\
\frac{\partial \loss}{\partial b}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

The way the optimisation search goes for $w$ and $b$ is that we typically assign them small random values $w_0$ and $b_0$ to begin with, and then update their values at each iteration $i$

$$
\begin{align}
w_{i+1} &= w_{i} - \eta \frac{\partial \loss}{\partial w}(w_i, b_i) \\
b_{i+1} &= b_{i} - \eta \frac{\partial \loss}{\partial b}(w_i, b_i)
\end{align}
$$

where $\eta > 0$ is a scaling factor we can also tune, typically known as the _learning rate_ of the algorithm.

For the perceptron, we know $\loss$ and hence we can calculate the gradient

$$
\nabla \loss(w,b) =
\begin{pmatrix}
\frac{\partial \loss}{\partial w} \\
\frac{\partial \loss}{\partial b}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{cases}
\begin{pmatrix}
- yx \\
- y
\end{pmatrix}
& \text{for mistakes} \\
\\
\begin{pmatrix}
0 \\
0
\end{pmatrix}
& \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

And finally 

$$
\begin{align}
w_{i+1} &= w_i + \eta yx \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}} \\
b_{i+1} &= b_i + \eta y \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}}
\end{align}
$$


## Interpreting the parameter updates

As we update $w$ and $b$ as proposed in the the previous section, the $z$ we assign to each sample $x$ also changes over time. 
Is there some rationale of how $z$ changes? 

To answer that, we can write 

$$
\begin{align}
z_{i+1} &= w_{i+1}x + b_{i+1} \\
&= (w_i + \eta yx \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}}) x + (b_i + \eta y \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}}) \\ 
&= w_i x + \eta yx^2 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}} + b_i + \eta y \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}} \\ 
&= (w_i x + b_i) + \eta y (1 + x^2) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}} \\
&= z_i + \eta y (1 + x^2) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}}
\end{align}
$$

And this allows us to exactly predict how $z$ changes from one iteration to the next

$$
z_{i + 1} - z_{i} = \eta y (1 + x^2) \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\text{mistake}}
$$

As we saw before, if there is no mistake, then nothing changes.
However, for mistakes:

* since $y \in \{-1, 1\}$,  the change in $z$ will always be of magnitude $\eta (1 + x^2)$, and;
* since $\eta > 0$ and $(1 + x^2) > 0$, $z$ increases or decreases depending only on the sign of $y$ 

This results is extremely interesting, let's focus on mistakes at iteration $i$ when $y = 1$.

If the model is mistaken, it means that $\hat{y} = -1$ which happens when $z_i < 0$.
Hence, as we update the parameters, we would love to get $z_{i + 1} > z_i$.

If we check the formula again, we can see that the update in $z_i$ will be positive because $y$ is positive.
The magintude of the change will be $\eta (1 + x^2)$.

For a given sample, $\eta (1 + x^2)$ is a constant, and thus $z$ changes in steps of a fixed size.
As the iterations pass by, as long as the sample is wrongly classified, the same update occurs on each iteration.

Eventually, at a final iteration $f$, we get $z_f \ge 0$.
The moment this happens, our prediction changes to $\hat{y} = 1$.
Since we now classify correctly the sample, then we can stop. 


The idea behind the perceptron is brute-forcing, a mindset of the type "if there is a solution, I will find it".


## Moving to higher order spaces

Finally, n the $z$-space, it always holds that $z$ is a scalar, i.e., $z \in \mathbb{R}$.
On the contrary, $x$, and thus $w$, could belong to an $n$-dimensional space, i.e. $x, w \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="30%"}
```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof w1       = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 0.5, step: 0.5, label: "w1"})
viewof w2       = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 0.5, step: 0.5, label: "w2"})
viewof b_2d     = Inputs.range([-8, 8], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "b_2d"})
viewof plotMode = Inputs.radio(["2D", "3D"], {value: "2D", label: "View"})
```
:::

::: {.column width="70%"}
::: {#fig-decision-boundary-2d}
{{< include ../_shared/decision-plot_2d.qmd >}}
Sigmoid classifier — adjust $w$ and $b$ to explore the decision boundary.
:::
:::

::::

Play with @fig-decision-boundary-2d.
